coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence sensor probe based on porous carbon, molecularly imprinted polymer and graphene quantum dots for the detection of trace sulfadimethoxine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Pongprom, Angkana
Chansud, Nutnicha
Bunkoed, Opas
coredata.dc:description: A hierarchical composite nanoprobe was fabricated and utilized to detect sulfadimethoxine as a specific and sensitive fluorescent sensor. The hierarchical composite probe integrated graphene quantum dots and porous carbon in molecularly imprinted polymer (GQDs@PC@MIP). The quantitative analysis of sulfadimethoxine was obtained by measuring the quenching of the fluorescence response of the nanoprobe when sulfadimethoxine bound with the selective recognition sites. The fluorescence emission of the composite nanoprobe decreased linearly from 0.10 to 25.0 μg L−1 and the limit of detection was 0.03 μg L−1. The recognition sites were highly specific for the sulfadimethoxine molecule with an imprinting factor of 8.75. The developed hierarchical composite nanoprobe was used to determine sulfadimethoxine in milk. Recoveries between 86.8 and 98.9% were achieved with RSDs lower than 5%. The developed nano-optosensor showed good accuracy, providing results that were in good agreement with HPLC analysis. However, the developed assay showed better sensitivity, required simple and cost-effective instruments and was more convenient to operate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfadimethoxine
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence sensor
Porous carbon

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of quantum dots and hierarchical composite fluorescence probe
Characterization of the related material and hierarchical composite sensing probe
Optimization of fabrication of hierarchical composite probe and detection condition
Effect of template (TP) to monomer (MN) to cross-linker (CL)
Effect of amount of porous carbon
Analysis of sulfadimethoxine in milk samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis strategy of the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe for sulfadimethoxine detection.

FT-IR spectra are of porous carbon (a) and composite GQDs@PC@MIP probe (b) (A). The SEM image (B) and N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms (C) are of porous carbon. The SEM image (D) and UV–Vis absorption (dot line) and fluorescence emission spectra (solid line) (E) are of GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe.

The charts show the effect of binding time (A) on fluorescence quenching, and the effect of dispersion solution pH (B), template to monomer to cross-linker ratio (C) and porous carbon loading (D) on the sensitivity of response toward sulfadimethoxine at concentrations of 0.10–25.0 μg L−1.
Sensitivity of different compositions of GQDs probes for the detection of sulfadimethoxine.

Fluorescence emission spectra were produced by the composite GQDs@PC@MIP (A) and GQDs@PC@NIP (B) nanoprobes in the presence of various concentrations of sulfadimethoxine. The calibration curves (C) show the response of the GQDs@PC@MIP and GQDs@PC@NIP probes to sulfadimethoxine in the concentration range of 0.0 to 25.0 μg L−1. The photograph (D) is of the composite GQDs@PC@MIP in the presence and absence of sulfadimethoxine under UV light.
The charts show the selectivity of the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe for sulfadimethoxine detection (A) and a competitive study of sulfadimethoxine against sulfathiazole (B).
Comparison of the determination of sulfadimethoxine by the developed optosensor and by HPLC.
Detection of sulfadimethoxine in milk using the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe.
The table presents a comparison of analytical performances of various methods for the determination of sulfadimethoxine.

head.ce:title: A fluorescence sensor probe based on porous carbon, molecularly imprinted polymer and graphene quantum dots for the detection of trace sulfadimethoxine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90110, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90110, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A hierarchical composite nanoprobe was fabricated and utilized to detect sulfadimethoxine as a specific and sensitive fluorescent sensor. The hierarchical composite probe integrated graphene quantum dots and porous carbon in molecularly imprinted polymer (GQDs@PC@MIP). The quantitative analysis of sulfadimethoxine was obtained by measuring the quenching of the fluorescence response of the nanoprobe when sulfadimethoxine bound with the selective recognition sites. The fluorescence emission of the composite nanoprobe decreased linearly from 0.10 to 25.0 μg L−1 and the limit of detection was 0.03 μg L−1. The recognition sites were highly specific for the sulfadimethoxine molecule with an imprinting factor of 8.75. The developed hierarchical composite nanoprobe was used to determine sulfadimethoxine in milk. Recoveries between 86.8 and 98.9% were achieved with RSDs lower than 5%. The developed nano-optosensor showed good accuracy, providing results that were in good agreement with HPLC analysis. However, the developed assay showed better sensitivity, required simple and cost-effective instruments and was more convenient to operate.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfadimethoxine is a sulfonamide antimicrobial widely utilized in veterinary medicine to treat bacterial infections of the urinary and respiratory tracts and soft tissue infections [1]. If overused, sulfadimethoxine accumulates in meat, milk and other products [2], where it poses a significant health risk for consumers. Since sulfadimethoxine is a source of public concern [3], the European Union (EU) has set the maximum residue limit (MRL) of sulfadimethoxine in meat at 100 μg kg−1 [4]. Thus, the development of a selective and sensitive assay for the detection of sulfadimethoxine is necessary. Several assays for sulfadimethoxine detection have previously been reported, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5], fluorescence sensing [6], chemiluminescence immunoassay [7] and electrochemical sensing [8].

Although HPLC is widely used method for the quantitative analysis of antibiotics, it requires large volumes of toxic solvent for the mobile phase, expensive instruments and long analysis time. Because of its versatility, rapidity, and low cost, spectrofluorimetry is a promising technique that can avoid these drawbacks. In addition, its sensitivity and selectivity can be enhanced using a sensitive and selective sensing probe. The sensitivity of spectrofluorimetry has been improved by employing the strong optical properties of quantum dots (QDs) [9]. Different types of QDs have been synthesized and applied as optical sensing probes. These QDs have included cadmium telluride QDs (CdTe QDs) [10], cadmium selenide QDs (CdSe QDs) [11], graphene QDs (GQDs) [12], nitrogen-doped QDs (N-QDs) [13] and carbon QDs (CQDs) [14,15]. GQDs have received considerable attention as an alternative material for the fabrication of highly sensitive fluorescent probes. They are simple to prepare, environmentally friendly and biocompatible, with low toxicity and low cost, and exhibit strong fluorescence intensity. GQDs have been applied as a sensing material for the detection of various compounds [16,17]. However, the direct detection of target analytes in complex samples using GQDs may be interfered by analogue structural compounds and other matrix substances. To improve the selectivity of sensing probes, GQDs can be integrated with highly specific materials to create a nanohybrid composite sensing probe. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are high affinity polymers compatible with a unique target analyte [18,19]. MIPs are complementary to the template molecules in shape, size and functional groups [20]. They are simple to prepare and stable [21]. MIPs have been applied as high specific materials in applications such as fluorescence sensor [22,23], electrochemiluminescence sensor [24], electrode modification [25,26] and solid phase adsorption [27,28]. To improve the mass transfer of an analyte to the recognition sites of a composite sensing probe, the inclusion of high affinity materials is an interesting approach. For the adsorption of sulfadimethoxine, porous carbon (PC) can facilitate the process since it can interact with sulfadimethoxine via π-π interaction and hydrogen bonding. PC has a large surface area which has been utilized to improve the extraction or removal of various compounds [29].

In this work, a hierarchical nanocomposite sensing probe was developed for sulfadimethoxine determination. GQDs and PC were integrated into an MIP (GQDs@PC@MIP). The synthesized hierarchical GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe was characterized, and the detection system and analytical performances were investigated. The developed sensing probe was used to determine sulfadimethoxine in spiked milk samples and its accuracy was evaluated with reference to the results of HPLC analysis.
The details of chemicals and materials can be found in the Supplementary Information (Sup. Inform).
The details of instruments are given in the Sup. Inform.

The preparation of PC was modified from a previous report [30]. Rice husks were brushed and washed repeatedly with DI water to eliminate inorganic compounds. The washed rice husks were dried at 100 °C for 12 h then pyrolyzed at 500 °C for 1 h under N2 to produce biochar husk. The silica and residual impurities in the biochar were removed in 1.0 M sodium hydroxide at 100 °C for 4 h with constant stirring. The purified biochar was rinsed with DI water until pH was nearly neutral and the biochar was then dried at 100 °C for 12 h. The dried biochar was impregnated with 1.0 M potassium hydroxide (biochar: KOH, mass proportion 1:3) and dried at 100 °C. The KOH-impregnated biochar was then pyrolyzed at 800 °C for 30 min under N2 at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. The pyrolized biochar was neutralized by adding 1.0 M HCl, filtered and then rinsed with hot water until pH was about 6.5–7.5, and lastly dried at 100 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of quantum dots and hierarchical composite fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were produced by pyrolysis of citric acid, Firstly, 2.00 g of citric acid were heated at 200 °C until they turned light yellow. Then, the liquid citric acid was dropped into 100 mL of 0.25 M NaOH and stirred for 30 min. The synthesized GQDs were purified in a dialysis bag for 24 h. The synthesis procedure of the GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The silica-embedded GQDs were prepared by mixing 375 μL of APTES and 5.0 mL of GQDs solution. The mixture solution was heated at 40 °C for 30 min under stirring. At the same time, 0.025 mmol sulfadimethoxine was dissolved in 5.0 mL of DI water, mixed with 35 μL of APTES and stirred for 1 h. The mixture solution was mixed with the silica-embedded GQDs-APTES solution under stirring for 30 min and 0.60 mg of porous carbon was added. The mixture was stirred for a further 30 min and then 400 μL of TEOS and 150 μL of NH3 were added to the mixture solution, which was stirred for 4 h to obtain the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobes. The template molecule (sulfadimethoxine) was removed in 10 mL ethanol under stirring for 10 min, followed by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The washing of the template was performed twice. A hierarchical nanocomposite non-imprinted polymer (GQDs@PC@NIP) was also prepared under the same conditions without the addition of the sulfadimethoxine.

The hierarchical GQDs@PC@MIP and GQDs@PC@NIP nanoprobes were dispersed in citrate–phosphate buffer at pH 7.0. The fluorescence intensity of 300 μL of each nanoprobe was measured in in the presence of 100 μL of sample solution or sulfadimethoxine standard after incubation for 16 min. Emission was measured from 400 to 680 nm from an excitation wavelength of 355 nm with a slit width of 10 nm.

Pretreatment of milk samples was performed according to a previous work with minor modification [31]. Briefly, 10.0 mL of milk sample were centrifuged at 5,000 rpm for 20 min to remove fat and then protein was removed by adding 10.0 mL of acetonitrile. Centrifugation was performed for 10 min at 5,000 rpm and the acetonitrile phase was collected and evaporated at 50 °C. The obtained sludge was dissolved in 10.00 mL ultrapure water and analyzed with an optosensor. Sulfadimethoxine in spiked milk was also determined by the optosensor to evaluate its accuracy. Accuracy was evaluated in terms of recovery, which was calculated according to the following equation; Recovery (%) = (CTotal – CReal/CSpiked) × 100; where, CTotal is the concentration of sulfadimethoxine found in the spiked sample, CReal is the concentration of sulfadimethoxine in the original real sample and CSpiked is the concentration of the standard sulfadimethoxine spiked in the real sample.

An Agilent 1100 HPLC system (Germany) was also used to determine sulfadimethoxine. The separation column was a Fortis C18 (150 mm 4.6 mm, 5.0 µm). The mobile phase was a mixture of acetonitrile and 0.10 % (v/v) formic acid (40:60 %v/v) passing at a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1. The wavelength of detection was 270 nm and the sample volume was 20 µL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the related material and hierarchical composite sensing probe
ce:section.ce:para:
The functional groups of PC and the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe were identified by FT-IR analysis (Fig. 2
A). In the FT-IR spectrum of PC, the peaks at 1590, 2950 and 3430 cm−1 were due to C = O, CH2 and OH stretching, respectively. The absorption peaks of PC were also present in the FT-IR spectrum of the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP probe along with a broad band at 1100 cm−1 that was due to Si-O-Si stretching of the MIP. The FT-IR results confirmed that the hierarchical composite sensing probe was successfully fabricated. An SEM image of PC (Fig. 2 B) showed the high porosity of the material. Its surface area was determined by the BET method. The surface area of PC was 1309.86 m2g−1and the pore size value was 1.738 nm. Fig. 2 C shows N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms of PC. The SEM image of the synthesized hierarchical GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe (Fig. 2 D) displays a uniform spherical morphology of GQDs@MIP particles with a rough surface coated on the surface of porous carbon. Fig. 2 E shows the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of hierarchical GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe. The developed composite sensing probe exhibited the high emission intensity necessary for a highly sensitive fluorescent probe for trace analysis. The quantum yields of the synthesized GQDs and hierarchical GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe, calculated using Rhodamine 6G as a reference, were 0.47 and 0.41 respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of fabrication of hierarchical composite probe and detection condition
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the best analytical sensitivity in a short analysis time, incubation time, dispersion solution pH, the ratio of template to monomer to cross-linker, and the loading of PC were investigated and optimized. The optimization of incubation time was based on fluorescence change (F0/F) and the optimization of the other parameters was based on the sensitivity of response of the probe toward sulfadimethoxine in the concentration range of 0.10–25.0 μg L−1. The optimization study was conducted at 25 °C for convenient analysis (room temperature).

Incubation time is the time required for adequate binding between the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe and sulfadimethoxine, and was examined from 2 to 30 min (Fig. 3
A). The change in fluorescence intensity (F0/F) increased with increasing incubation time up to 16 min and remained stable thereafter. Therefore, sulfadimethoxine and the sensing probe were incubated for 16 min before fluorescence was measured.

The sensitivity of the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP is pH dependent and, the pH of the dispersion solution affected the sensitivity of the probe toward sulfadimethoxine. Therefore, pH values that were too high or low affected the analytical results. The effect of the dispersion solution pH was evaluated in the range of 4.0–9.0 (Fig. 3 B). The GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe exhibited the highest sensitivity at pH 7.0. Sensitivity was lower at lower pH, since hydrogen ions in the solution could interrupt binding between recognition sites and sulfadimethoxine, weakening the hydrogen bond between sulfadimethoxine and APTES [32]. In addition, the pKa value of sulfadimethoxine is ∼ 6; at pH lower than the pKa, sulfadimethoxine is in protonated form and hydrogen bonding is weak. Sensitivity was also low at pH above 7.0, since hydroxyl ions were able to attack the surface of the probe, resulting in reduced binding between sulfadimethoxine and the GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe. In addition, in the alkaline condition, the silica shell is ionized and unstable. Therefore, the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP was dispersed in citrate–phosphate buffer at pH 7.0 before mixing with the sample solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of template (TP) to monomer (MN) to cross-linker (CL)
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the highest sensitivity of the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP nano probe, the effects of various mole ratios of TM to MN to CL were investigated. The highest sensitivity was achieved at a ratio of 1:6:70 (Fig. 3 C). Increasing the mole ratio of TM to MN increased sensitivity. The highest sensitivity was reached at a ratio of 1:6. Sensitivity was lower at a ratio of 1: 4 due to the low number of recognition sites. Sensitivity was also lower at 1: 8, due to the presence of excess monomer which hindered the formation of fully complementary recognition sites. When the ratio of TM to CL was investigated, the best sensitivity was achieved at the mole ratio of 1:70. Sensitivity was lower at the mole ratio of 1:65, probably due to unstable MIP networks caused by insufficient cross-linker. At 1:75, sensitivity was low because excess cross-linker could disturb the interaction between template and monomer. Therefore, the TM to MN to CL were combined at a mole ratio of 1:6:70 in the fabrication of the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of amount of porous carbon
ce:section.ce:para: Sulfadimethoxine produces π-π interactions with PC. The amount of PC in the composite GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe was varied from 0.40 to 1.0 mg. The PC content of 0.6 mg provided the highest sensitivity (Fig. 3 D). Sensitivity was lower at a PC content of 0.4 mg. Sensitivity was also lower at 0.8 and 1.0 mg since the excess PC obscured recognition cavities, resulting in fewer binding sites. Therefore, the PC content of 0.6 mg was selected for the preparation of the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe.

The sensitivity of various fluorescent probes for sulfadimethoxine detection were investigated, including a GQDs@NIP, a GQDs@PC@NIP, a GQDs@MIP and a GQDs@PC@MIP (Fig. 4 ). The GQDs@NIP nanoprobe exhibited the lowest sensitivity for sulfadimethoxine due to the absence of both specific recognition sites and adsorption materials. Incorporating PC into the composite nanoprobe improved sensitivity, significantly in the case of the two MIP sensing probes. The hierarchical GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe demonstrated the highest sensitivity. The presence of both specific recognition sites and adsorption materials resulted in greater and more rapid adsorption of the target analyte. Therefore, the nanocomposite GQDs@PC@MIP was utilized for the detection of sulfadimethoxine in real samples.

Before applying the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe to determine sulfadimethoxine in real samples, the analytical performances of the probe were investigated. The fluorescence emission spectra of the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP and GQDs@PC@NIP probes were recorded in the presence of sulfadimethoxine from 0.0 to 25.0 μg L−1. The emission response of the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP probe clearly decreased with increasing sulfadimethoxine, whereas the emission response of the GQDs@PC@NIP probe only slightly decreased at the corresponding sulfadimethoxine concentrations (Fig. 5
A and 5B). The target analyte could bind with specific recognition sites of the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP but there were no recognition sites in the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@NIP, so only non-specific adsorption occurred and, therefore, the fluorescence quenching intensity was small.

The hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe exhibited good linearity in the concentration range of 0.10–25.0 μg L−1 (Fig. 5 C). The linear equation was y (F0/F) = 0.1365XSulfadimethoxine + 1.3628 (R2 = 0.9932) and the limit of detection (LOD) (3σ/Slope) and limit of quantification (LOQ) (10σ/Slope) were 0.03 μg L−1 and 0.10 μg L−1, respectively. These values are much lower than the MRLs. A photograph of the composite GQDs@PC@MIP in the presence and absence of sulfadimethoxine under UV light is presented in Fig. 5 D.

The selectivity of the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP probe was assessed by testing with antibiotics of similar structures, including sulfathiazole, sulfadiazine, sulfamerazine and sulfamethazine. The sensitivity of the probe was higher toward sulfadimethoxine than the structural analogues (Fig. 6
A), indicating that the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP was highly selective toward sulfadimethoxine. Meanwhile, the sensitivity of the GQDs@PC@MIP and the GQDs@PC@NIP nanoprobes toward the structural analogues was not significantly different. A competitive study was conducted to confirm the specificity of the developed composite probe. The hierarchical GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe was used to detect sulfadimethoxine in the presence of different concentrations of sulfathiazole (Fig. 6 B). Raising sulfathiazole concentration had no effect on the response to sulfadimethoxine. The results indicated that the fabricated hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe was specific for sulfadimethoxine. The imprinting factor (IF), which was calculated from the ratio of Ksv of MIP to Ksv of NIP nanoprobe, was 8.75, confirming that many recognition sites were produced in the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe.

The stability of the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe was investigated in buffer solution at pH 7.0. The emission intensity did not significantly change within 300 min ( Fig. S3
A), which is enough time for analyte determination using spectrofluorimetry. The reproducibility of the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe was also investigated ( Fig. S3
B). Six different preparation lots were used to detect sulfadimethoxine and the RSD of average sensitivity was 2.0 %, indicating the good reproducibility of the fabrication process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of sulfadimethoxine in milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: Compared to previously reported methods, the developed optosensor using the hierarchical composite GQDs@PC@MIP nanoprobe exhibited better sensitivity. Recoveries and precision were better or comparable (Table 2 ). Compared to HPLC analysis, which is the most widely used analytical method for the detection of antibiotics, the performance of the optosensor was better in terms of sensitivity. Also, the cost of the present optosensing system is lower and the method does not require the organic solvents necessary for the mobile phase in an HPLC system. This optosensor exhibited good selectivity for sulfadimethoxine without using a separation column. However, if several target analytes in real sample need to be determined, chromatographic technique is more suitable due to the target analytes can be separated and identified using analytical column. The comparison indicated that the developed optosensor can be used as an accurate, sensitive and selective strategy for the detection of sulfadimethoxine at trace levels.

A nanooptosensor based on a hierarchical composite probe was developed and utilized for the detection of sulfadimethoxine. The hierarchical composite probe was designed by embedding graphene quantum dots and porous carbon in molecularly imprinted polymer (GQDs@PC@MIP). The high sensitivity and specificity of the fabricated nanoprobe enabled the detection of trace concentrations of sulfadimethoxine in milk. The developed hierarchical composite probe was applied as an optosensor to detect sulfadimethoxine in milk samples and good accuracy was achieved (recoveries between 86.8 and 98.9%). The analysis results of the fabricated optosensor and HPLC were in good agreement, but the optosensor had better sensitivity, was simpler to use and required less expensive equipment. The developed strategy can be modified for ultra-sensitive detection of other organic compound by changing template molecules and optimize some parameters for the determination of target analytes.

Angkana Pongprom: Visualization, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft. Nutnicha Chansud: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Opas Bunkoed: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT5-RSA63022-03), the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation. The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English proofreading.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2022.113812.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An amperometric aptasensor for ultrasensitive detection of sulfadimethoxine based on exonuclease-assisted target recycling and new signal tracer for amplification

Mu, An amperometric aptasensor for ultrasensitive detection of sulfadimethoxine based on exonuclease-assisted target recycling and new signal tracer for amplification, Biosens. Bioelectron. 117 (2018) 706-712. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2018.06.011.

De Backer, Detection of residues of tetracycline antibiotics in pork and chicken meat: correlation between results of screening and confirmatory tests, Analyst 123(12) (1998) 2737-41. https://doi.org/10.1039/a804909b.

A 1609 (2020) 460520. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chroma.2019.460520.

Anal. 91 (2020) 103526. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jfca.2020.103526.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical aptasensor for sulfadimethoxine detection based on the triggered cleavage activity of nuclease P1 by aptamer-target complex
